claim: 71% of lost dogs are found less than 1 mile from home; 14% found 1-5 miles away; 7% found more than 5 miles away.
stance_target: proximity-first search strategy — concentrate within 1-mile radius
scope_conditions: Montgomery County, Ohio, June–September 2005; n=187 lost dogs; biased sample (newspaper ads + animal agency contact)
quoted_support: 8% returned home, 71% found less than one mile, 14% were found 1-5 miles, and 7% were found more than 5 miles from home.
entities: Journal of the American Veterinary Medical Association

claim: Median recovery time for lost dogs is 2 days, with range from 0.5 to 21 days.
stance_target: first 48-72 hours are the critical intervention window for lost dog recovery
scope_conditions: Montgomery County, Ohio, June–September 2005; n=187 lost dogs; Lord et al. 2007b
quoted_support: Median time to recovery was 2 days (range, 0.5 to 21 days).
entities: Journal of the American Veterinary Medical Association

claim: Dogs with ID (tag/license/microchip) are recovered at a 53% rate versus 35% for dogs without ID.
stance_target: identification (microchip/tag) is a statistically significant recovery factor
scope_conditions: Montgomery County, Ohio, 2005; n=187 dogs; Lord et al. 2007b
quoted_support: 53% of dogs with ID were found vs 35% of dogs without ID.

claim: Dogs believed stolen are significantly less likely to be recovered despite equal search effort (hazard ratio 0.3 vs recovered dogs).
stance_target: theft classification as distinct behavioral scenario requiring different search protocol
scope_conditions: Montgomery County, Ohio, 2005; Lord et al. 2007b
quoted_support: Dogs believed stolen were less likely to be recovered, even though the search effort was not different.

claim: Published research shows only 11-16% of dogs go missing at least once in 5 years — the '1 in 3 pets go missing' statistic has no published source.
stance_target: 1-in-3 pets go missing claim attributed to ASPCA/AHA
quoted_support: The only published research study on lost pet rates found that between 11-16% of dogs and 12-18% of cats are likely to go missing at least once in five years.

claim: Posting neighborhood signs did not significantly improve dog recovery probability until after 7 days of the dog being missing.
stance_target: time-gated search strategy: agency contact and physical search first, signage most effective at day 7+
scope_conditions: Montgomery County, Ohio, 2005; Lord et al. 2007b; biased sample
quoted_support: Owners were significantly more likely to recover their lost dogs when they called an animal agency, visited, and posted signs in the neighborhood. However, posting signs did not become effective until the dog was lost for >7 days.

claim: Overall dog recovery rates across all published studies range 71-97%; US population estimate from Weiss 2012 is 86-97% (95% CI).
stance_target: high baseline probability of recovering a lost dog justifies intensive search investment
scope_conditions: US, multiple studies; Weiss 2012 CI applies to US population
quoted_support: Research studies found that between 71-97% of lost dogs were recovered. Weiss et al. (2012) used statistics to estimate that between 86 to 97% of lost dogs in the US are found.

claim: 46% of dogs in Lord 2007b were recovered via animal agency/shelter call or visit — the highest single method in that (shelter-biased) sample.
stance_target: relative importance of shelter reporting vs neighborhood search varies by sampling method
scope_conditions: Montgomery County Ohio, 2005, n=187; BIASED — only included owners who placed newspaper ads OR contacted animal agencies
quoted_support: Dogs were recovered primarily through a call or visit to an animal agency (46 [34.8%]), a dog license tag (24 [18.2%]), and posting of neighborhood signs (20 [15.2%]).
